inline_review:
commit ab87aa670969b28b089ca7c42fc249c8d84075c2
Author: Sergey Senozhatsky <senozhatsky@chromium.org>

zram: propagate read_from_bdev_async() errors

This commit updates read_from_bdev_async() to return an integer error code
instead of void. This allows memory allocation failures during async reads
to be propagated back to the caller for proper handling.

> diff --git a/drivers/block/zram/zram_drv.c b/drivers/block/zram/zram_drv.c
> --- a/drivers/block/zram/zram_drv.c
> +++ b/drivers/block/zram/zram_drv.c
> @@ -1445,21 +1445,21 @@ static void zram_async_read_endio(struct bio *bio)
>  	queue_work(system_highpri_wq, &req->work);
>  }
>  
> -static void read_from_bdev_async(struct zram *zram, struct page *page,
> -				 u32 index, unsigned long blk_idx,
> -				 struct bio *parent)
> +static int read_from_bdev_async(struct zram *zram, struct page *page,
> +				u32 index, unsigned long blk_idx,
> +				struct bio *parent)
>  {
>  	struct zram_rb_req *req;
>  	struct bio *bio;
>  
>  	req = kmalloc_obj(*req, GFP_NOIO);
>  	if (!req)
> -		return;
> +		return -ENOMEM;

When this -ENOMEM error propagates up through read_from_bdev() to
zram_read_page(), will it trigger an improper warning?

It appears zram_read_page() unconditionally evaluates if (WARN_ON(ret < 0))
and logs a decompression failure message. Emitting a kernel warning and stack
trace for a transient, expected memory allocation failure might cause issues,
especially on systems with panic_on_warn enabled.

Also, logging a decompression failure for a memory shortage seems factually
incorrect here. Should the error handling in zram_read_page() be updated to
handle -ENOMEM gracefully without emitting a stack trace?

>  
>  	bio = bio_alloc(zram->bdev, 1, parent->bi_opf, GFP_NOIO);

While looking at the use of parent->bi_opf here, could this lead to a
use-after-free or data corruption during asynchronous IO?

If zram_bvec_write_partial() calls zram_read_page(..., bio) during a
read-modify-write cycle and passes the parent write bio instead of NULL,
read_from_bdev() would forward execution here. This would create a new bio
using the parent's REQ_OP_WRITE, erroneously issuing a write to the backing
device instead of a read, overwriting the backing device with an
uninitialized page buffer.

Since this returns 0 immediately after submission, zram_bvec_write_partial()
would believe the read completed synchronously, copy partial user data,
write it to zram, and immediately free the page via __free_page(page).
The asynchronous write bio would remain in-flight, performing DMA using the
now-freed page and potentially corrupting buddy allocator memory later when
the completion handler executes memset_page(page, 0).

Should zram_bvec_write_partial() pass NULL instead of bio to force a
synchronous read?

>  	if (!bio) {
>  		kfree(req);
> -		return;
> +		return -ENOMEM;
>  	}

Is this error handling path reachable?

Because bio_alloc() on a block device utilizes the mempool-backed fs_bio_set,
and the GFP_NOIO flag implicitly includes __GFP_DIRECT_RECLAIM, the mempool
allocator will block and retry until a bio is available. It seems it cannot
fail or return NULL in this context.

[ ... ]
output:
{"findings":[{"problem":"Propagating `-ENOMEM` from `read_from_bdev_async()` triggers an improper `WARN_ON` and incorrect error log in `zram_read_page()`.","severity":"Medium","severity_explanation":"The patch updates `read_from_bdev_async()` to return `-ENOMEM` when `kmalloc_obj(*req, GFP_NOIO)` fails. This error propagates up through `read_from_bdev()` to `zram_read_page()`, which unconditionally evaluates `if (WARN_ON(ret < 0))` and logs `pr_err("Decompression failed! err=%d, page=%u\n", ret, index)`. Triggering a kernel warning (which can panic systems with `panic_on_warn` enabled) for a transient, expected memory allocation failure is an anti-pattern. Furthermore, the error log 'Decompression failed!' is factually incorrect for a memory shortage. The error handling in `zram_read_page()` must be updated to handle `-ENOMEM` gracefully without emitting a stack trace."},{"problem":"Pre-existing bug: `zram_bvec_write_partial()` incorrectly passes a WRITE bio to `zram_read_page()`, causing severe data corruption and a Use-After-Free during asynchronous IO.","severity":"Critical","severity_explanation":"While analyzing the modified `parent->bi_opf` propagation, a critical pre-existing bug was identified. When performing a read-modify-write cycle, `zram_bvec_write_partial()` calls `zram_read_page(..., bio)` and passes the parent WRITE bio instead of `NULL`. Because `parent` is not NULL, `read_from_bdev()` forwards execution to `read_from_bdev_async()`.

1. The async function creates a new bio using the parent's `bi_opf` (`REQ_OP_WRITE`), which erroneously issues a WRITE to the backing device instead of a READ, overwriting the backing device with the uninitialized `page` buffer.
2. `read_from_bdev_async()` returns `0` immediately after submission.
3. Believing the read completed synchronously, `zram_bvec_write_partial()` copies partial user data into the uninitialized `page`, writes it to ZRAM, and immediately frees the page via `__free_page(page)`.
4. The asynchronous WRITE bio remains in-flight, performing DMA using the now-freed page, resulting in a Use-After-Free and leaking uninitialized memory to disk. Later, the async completion handler triggers `decompress_bdev_page()`, which executes `memset_page(page, 0)` on the freed page, corrupting buddy allocator memory.

To fix this, `zram_bvec_write_partial()` must pass `NULL` instead of `bio` to force a synchronous read, matching the correct behavior seen in `zram_bvec_read_partial()`."},{"problem":"The error handling path for `bio_alloc()` returning NULL is mathematically unreachable (dead code).","severity":"Low","severity_explanation":"The patch adds a failure path returning `-ENOMEM` if `bio_alloc(zram->bdev, 1, parent->bi_opf, GFP_NOIO)` returns NULL. According to block layer mempool allocation guarantees, `bio_alloc()` on a block device utilizes the mempool-backed `fs_bio_set`. Because the `GFP_NOIO` flag implicitly includes `__GFP_DIRECT_RECLAIM`, the mempool allocator will block and retry until a bio is available; it cannot fail or return NULL. Therefore, the `if (!bio)` check and its associated error return path are dead code."}],"fixes":"","review_inline":"commit ab87aa670969b28b089ca7c42fc249c8d84075c2
